sanskrit_devanagari:
नादत्ते कस्यचित्पापं न चैव सुकृतं विभुः ।
अज्ञानेनावृतं ज्ञानं तेन मुह्यन्ति जन्तवः ॥ १५ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
nādatte kasyacit pāpaṁ na caiva sukṛtaṁ vibhuḥ |
ajñānenāvṛtaṁ jñānaṁ tena muhyanti jantavaḥ || 15 ||

translations.verbatim_quote: Nor does the Supreme Lord assume anyone’s sinful or pious activities. Embodied beings, however, are bewildered because of the ignorance which covers their real knowledge.
Nor does the Supreme Lord assume anyone’s sinful or pious activities. Embodied beings, however, are bewildered because of the ignorance which covers their real knowledge.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 3184 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: The Sanskrit word vibhu means the Supreme Lord who is full of unlimited knowledge, riches, strength, fame, beauty and renunciation. He is always satisfied in Himself, undisturbed by sinful or pious activities.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 5.15 Vibhuh, the Omnipresent; na adatte, neither accetps; kasyacit, anybody’s-even a adevotee’s; papam, sin; na ca eva, nor even; does He accept sukrtam, virtue offered by devotees. Why then are such virtuous acts as worship etc. as also sacrifices, charity, oblation, etc. worship etc.
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-5-15-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Adi Shankaracharya of Advaita Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara: the vibhu (the all-pervading Self / brahman) does not take on anyone's sin or virtue. Knowledge (jñāna, the awareness of the Self) is veiled by ignorance (ajñāna / avidyā), and beings, deluded by this veiling, falsely attribute agency and karmic accumulation to the Self. Shankara's Advaita reading: the Self never accumulates karma; the appearance of karma-accumulation is a function of avidyā.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-5-15-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja: the vibhu (the all-pervading, here read as the Lord or as the self in its essential nature, depending on context) does not take on anyone's pāpa or puṇya. Knowledge is veiled by ignorance, and beings are deluded — they attribute agency and fruit-coupling to the self that does not in essence have them. The verse establishes that fruit-of-action belongs to the karma-context (svabhāva), not to the self/Lord.

positions.rendering: the all-pervading Self / brahman; does not accumulate karma because it does not act
the all-pervading (Lord or self in essential nature); does not take on karma because karma belongs to the karmic conjunction

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Sectarian disagreement on the referent of vibhu. Shankara reads it as the Self/brahman; Ramanuja can read it as the Lord or the jīva-in-essential-nature; Prabhupada reads it as the Supreme Lord. All three preserve the structural claim: the vibhu does not take on pāpa/puṇya; what appears as karmic accumulation is a function of ignorance.

literal_meaning: The all-pervading takes on neither anyone's sin nor anyone's virtue. Knowledge is veiled by ignorance; by this beings are deluded.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna closes the metaphysical block. The vibhu (the all-pervading) does not take on anyone's pāpa (sin) or sukṛta (virtue). What appears as karma-accumulation is the veiling of jñāna by ajñāna — beings (jantavaḥ) are deluded by this veiling. The verse sets up the diagnosis that 5.16 will resolve: knowledge dispels the ignorance, and the diagnostic of mistaken agency dissolves.
